technicalDetails.detail: OPS together with Optimal Power Synergy India have completed a new solar PV, micro-grid system in the Andaman Islands.
The connected power sources include 75kWp of solar PV, a 240V, 2400Ah battery bank and 2 diesel generator sets rated to 30kVA each.
OPS India have supplied two proprietary technology parallel inverter modules rated at 20kVA each, with a Station Control Module and other balance of system, power electronic components.
The Station Control Module employs specialised control software and will take suitable actions, based on inbuilt intelligence, to ensure reliable and optimum operation of the hybrid power plant.
The Station Controller also provides remote monitoring of various system parameters such as battery status, loads, diesel genset operation.
This micro-grid system provides excellent power reliability and redundancy as well as significant diesel fuel savings.

baikampady-mangalore-microgrid.summary: SELCO India commissioned a small direct-current solar microgrid in April 2014 for a 75-home informal labor colony in the Baikampady industrial estate north of Mangalore. The documented design combined 1.2 kWp of PV, a nominal 19.2 kWh battery bank, grid-assisted charging, and 96 VDC distribution for efficient lighting, phone charging, three fans, and one television. Canara Bank financed the system through a loan to the property owner, who collected service payments with rent. Public evidence located in this review documents implementation through 2015 but not the system's later operating condition.
baikampady-mangalore-microgrid.status: Commissioned in April 2014 and documented as implemented in a 2015 SELCO site summary. No reliable independent post-2015 operating or decommissioning evidence was found through July 2026, so present operating status is unknown.

banggi-island.summary: Sabah Electricity's Banggi Island station is a solar-battery-diesel hybrid supplying homes, schools, and businesses on the island. Current owner documentation gives 1,200 kWp of PV, a 720-cell gel battery bank, and eight diesel generators ranging from 200 kW to 1,000 kW against an approximately 950 kW served load. A Leonics engineering presentation for the project records a 1,200 kWp array, 2,075 kW of inverter capacity, a 2,880 kWh main battery bank, and 1,650 kVA of diesel capacity. The station has received battery, inverter, and diesel upgrades and remains in service, but no source found provides a current annual renewable-energy percentage.
banggi-island.status: Operating under Sabah Electricity as of the owner's current ESG account reviewed in July 2026. That account says battery and inverter upgrades and added diesel capacity have sustained performance. Commissioning dates conflict by phase/source: TNB describes commissioning in 2013, while Leonics records commercial operation on 25 January 2014 and operation since February 2014.

bario.summary: The Bario Central Solar Hybrid Power System supplies the remote Bario Highlands with an off-grid combination of PV, battery storage, and diesel backup. Sarawak Energy records construction beginning in 2010, initial commissioning in September 2015, transfer to its operation in June 2016, and an October 2016 official launch. The launch-era rating was 887 kW and at least 70% of generated energy was expected from solar. Vendor documents preserve design changes around 885.84-906 kWp of PV, 3,860 kWh of battery storage, and varying inverter/diesel totals. Sarawak's current Rural Electrification Masterplan describes a 998 kW station serving more than 400 households and proposes a future 33 kV grid interconnection.
bario.status: Operating as an off-grid solar-battery-diesel station under Sarawak Energy in the current Rural Electrification Masterplan reviewed in July 2026. The plan proposes interconnecting Bario to the state grid via an approximately 60 km Ba'kelalan-Bario line and 33 kV substation by 2030; that connection is not yet documented as complete.

technicalDetails.detail: Published engineering analysis describes a mixed-bus system: PV and batteries feed a DC bus through converters while diesel generators feed the AC bus. In the modeled load-following logic, PV serves load and charges storage; the battery discharges to its minimum state of charge before diesel is dispatched.
Construction began in 2010, first commissioning occurred in September 2015, Sarawak Energy accepted operation in June 2016, and the official launch was held on 25 October 2016.

The central plant initially covered nine villages. Two extension villages were expected to lift the central-plant reach above 300 homes, while five other small nearby settlements were assigned separate stand-alone hybrid systems and should not be counted as central-plant capacity.

Sarawak's Rural Electrification Masterplan proposes a 33 kV substation and roughly 60 km covered-conductor link from Ba'kelalan, interconnecting rather than simply replacing the hybrid station under the 2030 plan.

The 887 kW launch rating, 885.84/906 kWp vendor PV values, and 998 kW current masterplan value belong to different dated descriptions. They are retained separately rather than normalized into a single undocumented capacity.

baron-techno-park.summary: Baron Techno Park is an off-grid renewable-energy research and demonstration site at Parang Racuk, Gunungkidul. Its documented hybrid plant combined 36 kW of PV, two wind turbines totaling 15 kW, nominal 25 kW diesel and 25 kW biodiesel generators, a battery bank reported as 288 kVAh/1,200 Ah, and a 25 kVA hybrid controller. The available plant changed over time: a 2017 study said only 24 kW of PV and 5 kW of wind were operating, while a 2023 engineering paper describes a current 36 kW PV/5 kW wind/20 kWh battery/25 kW inverter configuration and reports that the Fortis wind turbine had been stopped by a 2019 malfunction.
baron-techno-park.status: Continues to be documented as an off-grid research/test microgrid. The 5 kW Fortis wind subsystem had been out of service since a 2019 malfunction and was planned for reinstallation in the 2023 paper; no later public confirmation of its return to service was found through July 2026. PV/battery operation is described in 2023, but a recent measured generation mix was not located.

technicalDetails.detail: PV, wind, biodiesel/diesel, battery storage, and inverter resources connect through an off-grid hybrid controller with data acquisition. The site is primarily a demonstration and research platform rather than a utility-scale supply plant.
The 2017 performance study says obstacles left only 24 kW of the 36 kW PV inventory and 5 kW of the 15 kW wind inventory operating at the study time.
The Fortis 5 kW turbine stopped operating in 2019 due to malfunction. The 2023 paper says reinstallation was planned in the near future, but the review found no later commissioning confirmation.
A 2015 site account says the biodiesel demonstration shifted from unsuccessful jatropha feedstock toward used cooking oil; the installation was a pilot/research line, not commercial mass production.
Large annual generation and energy-mix figures in the 2017 paper are HOMER-model results, not metered operating production, and are therefore not presented as an observed energy mix here.

blue-lake-rancheria-microgrid.summary: Blue Lake Rancheria's main community microgrid has operated since 2017 with a 420 kW SolarWorld/SMA PV plant, a 500 kW/950 kWh Tesla Powerpack battery system through two Dynapower inverters, a Siemens Spectrum Power microgrid controller, and a 1 MW Cummins diesel generator. It has islanded through grid outages and supported the surrounding community during disasters. A second Play Station 777 microgrid entered service in 2020, a new Tesla Megapack installation was commissioned in 2024 without a published capacity, and a $5 million 2025 California Energy Commission award is funding a nested 12 kV Microgrid 2.0 expansion.
blue-lake-rancheria-microgrid.status: The 2017 main microgrid and 2020 Play Station 777 microgrid are operational. The tribe reports commissioning an additional Tesla Megapack installation in 2024, but does not disclose its power or energy capacity. The nested Microgrid 2.0 expansion was funded in June 2025 and remained an active demonstration/expansion effort in 2026; it should not yet be counted as commissioned capacity.

technicalDetails.detail: Biomass-gasification-fuel cell system that turns biomass into electricity.
The 175 kilowatt (kW) system is fueled by biomass (plant-based biological material) that has been reduced to sawdust size.

The original project finished on schedule with less than 1.5% cost overrun. It saved approximately $160,000 in 2017 and was expected to save about $200,000 annually thereafter while avoiding 159 metric tons CO2e in 2017 (about 175 tons expected annually).

The main system islanded seamlessly during a 2017 outage. Both tribal microgrids remained operational during a three-day 2019 utility outage, and the tribe reports supporting thousands of people after the December 2022 magnitude-6.4 earthquake.

A $5 million CEC award announced 11 June 2025 funds advanced 12 kV nested controls/protection, circuit reconfiguration, and added solar/storage across three facility microgrids, with PG&E invited to testing. Equipment vendors and final capacities were not fixed in the public scope and are not assigned here.
The nested project is intended to inform replication along the approximately 142-mile Hoopa 1101 feeder serving Hoopa Valley, Yurok, and Karuk communities.

The CEC final report for the commissioned 2017 system identifies PV, BESS, grid, and a diesel generator. It does not include the 175 kW biomass/fuel-cell concept found in older planning material; that concept should not be counted as installed main-microgrid capacity.
The tribe hosted an operating-microgrid tour in April 2026 and described ongoing TERAS and nested-microgrid expansion work, providing current evidence that the resilience program remains active.

bonaire-microgrid.summary: Bonaire's island power system is an operating hybrid of 11.1 MW of wind, 5.3 MW of solar, 28.5 MW of thermal generation, and a battery fleet currently described by ContourGlobal as 14 MW. The original 2010 renewable build used twelve Enercon E-44/900 turbines, one E-33/330 turbine, MAN diesel engines, and a 3 MW Saft nickel-cadmium battery. Wärtsilä added a 6 MW/6 MWh battery and GEMS controls in 2019, and ContourGlobal added a further battery and the 5.3 MW solar plant in 2023. The system remains the island's sole bulk supply to distributor WEB, although 2024 demand growth, extreme heat, and cable damage caused outages and prompted capacity and grid upgrades.
bonaire-microgrid.status: Operating under ContourGlobal, with WEB distributing electricity. Current owner figures are 28.5 MW thermal, 11.1 MW wind, 14 MW BESS power, and 5.3 MW solar. WEB reported 2024 reliability problems and added 5 MW of production capacity while upgrading controls and transmission. Wind repowering/expansion is planned, but the publicly stated 60% renewable target was not verified as achieved and no current audited annual energy mix was found.

The battery array is normally held at 90% state-of-charge so it can be brought on line as an artificial load when necessary.

Wind: (11) 1 MW Enercon wind turbines

Wind and solar are dispatched with battery storage and thermal generators on a single island grid. The batteries provide fast frequency/power-quality support, absorb excess renewable output, and bridge the interval while diesel units start.

Wärtsilä reports that the 6 MW/6 MWh addition reduced CO2 emissions by 8%, fossil-fuel use by 5%, eliminated renewable curtailment, and increased annual wind utilization by 6-8%. Load-rejection tests demonstrated continued operation after loss of wind, engines, or load without customer shedding.

ContourGlobal's current report describes the additional battery as commercially operating in April 2023 and the 5.3 MW PV plant later in 2023. Local reporting places battery commissioning in July and solar commissioning in September; the difference likely reflects commercial-operation versus local handover milestones.

WEB attributed recent outages to an unexpected 15-20% demand increase, extreme heat, and cable damage. It reported adding 5 MW of production capacity, digitizing controls, and advancing transmission upgrades with a January 2025 target.

ContourGlobal says it is pursuing wind repowering that would more than double wind capacity. WEB also identifies wind-farm expansion in its 2024-2030 transition planning; no completed repower rating was located.

The current 14 MW BESS figure is a power rating. It must not be converted to 14 MWh or simply added to historical energy ratings because the owner does not publish the current aggregate duration/energy capacity.

bornholm-island.summary: Bornholm is a live island power system and long-running smart-grid test platform connected to Sweden by one subsea cable. EcoGrid EU enrolled roughly 2,000 households in 2011-2015, and EcoGrid 2.0 concluded in 2019 after remotely coordinating electric heating in 800 homes. Under normal conditions BEOF says its own local generation is entirely wind, solar, biomass, and biogas, while the island imports Nordic electricity when local renewable output is insufficient and starts Rønne thermal units during cable outages. BEOF announced a 10 MW Hitachi Energy battery for installation in the old Rønne coal plant in 2026, but no public commissioning confirmation was found by this review.
bornholm-island.status: EcoGrid EU and EcoGrid 2.0 are complete demonstrations; Bornholm remains an operating island grid and active Test Island Bornholm platform. BEOF and Energinet signed a May 2026 reserve-supply agreement for 2027-2031. A 10 MW Hitachi Energy battery was announced for 2026 installation but is not counted as commissioned absent confirmation. The separate 3 GW Bornholm Energy Island offshore project was revived by a January 2026 Danish-German agreement and remains future infrastructure.

Bornholm is connected to the Swedish/Nordic grid by a single subsea cable. When that connection is unavailable, BEOF maintains island-operation capability using local reserve plant.

The 2016-2019 demonstration remotely coordinated heat pumps and electric heaters in 800 homes to test flexible demand against grid and market needs. The earlier target of 1,000 devices/households should not replace the realized 800-household figure.
BEOF reports more than ten years as a full-scale test island, a portfolio valued around DKK650 million, and more than EUR100 million of project activity handled.

The May 2026 BEOF-Energinet agreement requires BEOF to maintain reserve generation and response for 2027-2031 when the Sweden cable is unavailable, replacing the arrangement that runs through the end of 2026.
BEOF announced the 10 MW battery on 13 March 2025 for installation during 2026. No public commissioning confirmation was found by 20 July 2026, so it remains classified as announced/in installation.

The January 2026 Danish-German agreement revived the separate 3 GW Bornholm Energy Island offshore transmission project. It is future interconnector/offshore-wind infrastructure and must not be added to the current island microgrid fleet.

borrego-microgrid.summary: SDG&E's Borrego Springs community microgrid serves an isolated desert feeder with extensive local solar, batteries, an ultracapacitor, diesel backup, and advanced islanding controls. The original system supported thousands of customers during major 2013 outages. A major Microgrid 3.0 upgrade replaces two 1.8 MW diesels with two 2.5 MW Tier IV Final units and adds six Tesla battery containers totaling 7 MW/14 MWh. Replacement generators were commissioned in March 2025, while June 2025 community minutes say the battery was still commissioning and local/remote control integration remained unfinished. DOE/NREL work nevertheless completed field validation of renewable-led black start and islanding using a grid-forming battery and PXiSE controller.
borrego-microgrid.status: Operating community microgrid in a major upgrade/integration phase. The two 2.5 MW replacement diesels were commissioned and functional by March 2025. The 7 MW/14 MWh Tesla BESS was still commissioning on 4 June 2025, with local controls targeted for September and remote integration by year-end; no later official completion confirmation was found through July 2026. A proposed 250 kW, 16-hour hydrogen resource also remains unverified as commissioned.

The microgrid normally remains grid-connected/dormant while local solar exports. During daytime island operation it can serve the whole community; at night it prioritizes critical loads and can shed noncritical load according to available battery and fuel.
After an April 2013 windstorm the microgrid served 1,225 customers for about six hours. During a September 2013 storm that downed 20 poles, it served 1,060 customers for roughly 25 hours.

CEC's 2024 review records several successful planned outages but also failed islanding demonstrations caused by controller-communications and frequency-stabilization problems, showing that nameplate capacity alone did not guarantee successful transitions.

Community minutes report that both legacy generators failed during a 61-hour planned February 2023 microgrid operation, taking the system out of service. The replacement generators were commissioned by March 2025.

The DOE/NREL project completed modeling, controller/hardware-in-the-loop work, and field tests in which a grid-forming battery led black start and islanding instead of diesel. Synchrophasor and power-quality measurements verified the fast response, and the DERMS interface was replicated at other SDG&E sites.

SDG&E's 2024 fact sheet expected completion in Q2 2025, but 4 June 2025 minutes say batteries were still commissioning, local controls were targeted for September, and remote control-center integration for year-end. The later direct evidence supersedes the forecast.

The CPUC describes planned CAISO market participation for the upgraded assets; it also says the then-current microgrid did not provide resource adequacy. This is a plan, not verified operating revenue/service.

bosch-dc-microgrid-california-honda-faci.summary: California Energy Commission grant EPC-14-053 funded Bosch's 380 V direct-current building microgrid demonstration at an American Honda Motor Co. warehouse in Chino, California. The final installation coupled 286 kW of rooftop solar, a 180 kW/540 kWh lithium-ion battery, Bosch energy management, DC lighting, fan drives, and forklift charging on a common DC bus. CEC's 2024 retrospective says the system was removed after the grant because Bosch discontinued the commercial offering.

technicalDetails.detail: The buildings solar PV arrays, DC ventalation system, DC lighting, and DC energy storage will form a microgrid on a 380-volt DC bus.
The building-scale system avoided repeated DC-to-AC-to-DC conversions by linking rooftop PV, battery storage, and compatible building loads on a 380 V DC bus while retaining utility service.

CEC reported 2-6% DC-load efficiency improvement, 7-10% greater solar utilization, and an 8% storage-efficiency improvement; the 2019 EPIC summary reported 3-5% overall efficiency improvement for lighting, ventilation, and forklift charging.
The system supported designated critical loads during outage testing and avoided use of the site's diesel backup generator.

CEC estimated roughly 30% lower total cost of ownership at commercial scale; the host facility manager estimated 45-50% lower maintenance cost, but CEC cautioned that project delays left limited baseline and operating data.

Flow-battery supplier failure, interconnection delays, a separately managed rooftop-solar project, and rate changes delayed the work. After the grant, Bosch discontinued the product offering and the demonstration equipment was uninstalled, so the project did not proceed to a commercial launch.
Current CEC and PNNL project records place the host warehouse in Chino, California, not Rancho Cordova.

brevoort-co-op-microgrid.summary: The Brevoort cooperative at 11 Fifth Avenue in Manhattan installed a 400 kW natural-gas combined-heat-and-power microgrid built from four 100 kW Tecogen InVerde modules. Commissioned in 2010, it normally operated in parallel with Con Edison under zero-export control and islanded for six days after Hurricane Sandy, serving electricity, heat, domestic hot water, elevators, and other building loads.

technicalDetails.detail: Cogeneration saves $300,000 / year
In normal grid-parallel operation the controls maintained at least 50 kW of utility import and prohibited export; typically three engines ran in winter and all four in summer.

UL 1741-certified CERTS logic let modules share load autonomously without a master controller. A utility-protection panel detected grid failure, opened the service breaker, and initiated an island restart.

The automatic utility breaker did not operate during Sandy, so the superintendent manually isolated the building and restarted the CHP. The building then ran islanded for six days, supporting residents and temporary guests with electricity, heat, water, and elevators.
The IEEE performance paper calculated as much as 92% total fuel utilization when all recoverable heat was used.
The IEEE analysis calculated $228,871 of 2013 energy savings on a 2007-cost basis and expected payback under five years; EPA reported approximately $300,000 annual realized savings.
This record is the Brevoort at 11 Fifth Avenue and should not be combined with the separate Brevoort East cooperative project, which public databases list with different CHP capacity.

brookhaven-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: Brookhaven's Independence Hill Microgrid was a NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility design linking Brookhaven Town Hall and Sachem High School East on Long Island. The preferred concept combined two new natural-gas CHP plants, two existing 10 kW wind turbines, existing diesel backup, and a proposed battery over an underground interconnection. The study did not establish that the project was built.

technicalDetails.detail: Technology would include solar panels and two existing 10 kW wind turbines, fuel cells, battery storage, and/or microturbines powered by the onsite wastewater treatment plant.

The project would incorporate sophisticated monitoring software operated by Brookhaven National Laboratory that will maximize the cost-benefit of the electrical generation and storage capacity components of the microgrid.
Technology would include solar panels and two existing 10 kW wind turbines, fuel cells, battery storage, and/or microturbines powered by the onsite wastewater treatment plant.

The two facilities would be joined by an underground distribution cable and normally operate grid-connected without exporting power. Controls were intended to detect outages, island automatically, prioritize renewable generation, shed noncritical loads, black-start, and reconnect after utility restoration.
The study modeled annual CHP output of 5,608 MWh at Town Hall and 3,350 MWh at the school, plus 22 MWh per year from each wind turbine. Diesel generation was reserved for outage operation.

The preferred design was modeled to cover 98% of annual electric needs and assumed seven days of natural-gas availability during a major outage; these were feasibility assumptions, not measured results.
The study projected $810,352 in annual energy savings, approximately $140,000 annual operating and maintenance cost, and about a 15-year simple payback.

The feasibility model reduced annual carbon dioxide at both sites but increased NOx and particulate emissions under its uncontrolled natural-gas CHP assumptions, illustrating that the proposal was not an all-renewable design.
Tecumseh Elementary School was evaluated initially and then removed because the district planned to close it; the final study covered Town Hall and Sachem High School East only.

NYSERDA describes its published Stage 2 list as complete, and Independence Hill is not present. The conclusion that it stopped after Stage 1 is therefore a program-record inference rather than an explicit cancellation notice.

brooklyn-microgrid.summary: Brooklyn Microgrid was LO3 Energy's peer-to-peer local-energy trading pilot in Brooklyn, New York. Participants used custom meters and the Exergy blockchain platform to transact attributes associated with rooftop solar while electricity continued to flow over Con Edison's distribution system. A physical ten-block islandable microgrid was discussed, but public evidence documents a virtual marketplace pilot rather than a completed electrical island.
brooklyn-microgrid.status: Pilot no longer operating by 2025. Public activity is documented from the first 2016 trades through a 2019 regulatory-sandbox effort; the project site stopped updating in 2019 and LO3's site later disappeared.

technicalDetails.detail: Users could bid according to price, fuel source, and other energy attributes through an application. Early transactions represented local solar value while financial settlement and actual power delivery still depended on conventional systems and Con Edison wires.

The demonstrated system was a virtual peer-to-peer marketplace. Islanding a ten-block section to support a high school, medical facilities, and other critical loads was a future plan, not a documented completed capability.
New York rules did not generally permit non-utilities to sell electricity directly. The 2019 sandbox therefore focused on trading energy attributes while regulators evaluated a broader framework.

A 2025 Electrosuisse follow-up found the project had quietly ended: brooklyn.energy had not been updated since 2019, LO3's website was gone, and an early participant confirmed the marketplace was no longer operating. His solar array remained an ordinary Con Edison-interconnected system.
The first demonstration was also called TransActive Grid; later public materials used Brooklyn Microgrid and BMG.

technicalDetails.detail: At the heart of the Microgrid is a vanadium redoxflow battery supplied by GILDEMEISTER energy storage GmbH.
The battery is connected to the DC bus by built-in ARDA Battery DC-DC Converters.
​The Energy Storage system maintains the DC bus voltage and controls the Energy Management function of the Microgrid.
The distributed generation components linked to the DC bus will include 16KW of solar and 10KW of gas generation.
Both Sources will be integrated into the DC Microgrid using the ARDA Source DC-DC Converters, which in the case of Solar include maximum power point tracking (MPPT) .

The battery energy-storage converter forms and regulates the DC bus. Sources and loads respond locally to bus voltage, allowing real-time coordination without communications; supervisory communications are used for optimization, monitoring, and data collection.
ARDA describes the platform as capable of seamless transition between grid-connected and islanded operation, with the battery remaining the bus-voltage reference in both modes.
A PLC could shed or dim loads based on system conditions while bidirectional interfaces supported AC and DC loads on the common bus.

Gentherm acquired Etratech in November 2017 and said it would continue operations at the Burlington headquarters; this establishes a host ownership change, not the microgrid's current operating status.

It received a DoD Platform IT Risk Approval/Authorization to Operate in August 2015, reported as the first DoD microgrid-control cybersecurity accreditation.

DOE's final report states that all three SPIDERS phases successfully operated their microgrids and met the joint demonstration goals; it does not publish a Camp Smith-specific post-2015 fuel-savings data series.

technicalDetails.detail: Sandia's 2017 portfolio explicitly lists Cannon AFB as a FY2011 DOE Office of Electricity/DoD conceptual design or assessment under 1 MW, while operational prototypes and large demonstrations are shown in separate categories.
A 2010 Sandia presentation listed Cannon among installations expressing FY2011 project interest; the later 2017 portfolio confirms an assessment occurred but provides no evidence of implementation.

The Air Force/DLA Energy awarded Ameresco an ESPC in June 2021 covering five conservation measures across 119 buildings and a 1.9 MW PV array. Announcements targeted Q1 2023 completion, but the reviewed official sources do not confirm final completion or describe the project as an islandable microgrid.

On June 4, 2026 the Air Force demonstrated Prometheus Hydrogen's HEGO backup system for Cannon's water supply. The solid-state hydrogen system is being evaluated as an alternative to mobile and stationary diesel backup generators; the Air Force calls it a pilot, not the FY2011 microgrid.

As of June 2026 Cannon still relied on mobile and stationary diesel generators for outage backup and was piloting hydrogen for one water-supply application. Public sources do not establish a base-wide islandable microgrid or storage fleet.
The FY2011 conceptual microgrid, the 2021 solar/controls ESPC, and the 2026 water-system hydrogen backup pilot are three distinct efforts and should not have their capacities or status merged.

technicalDetails.detail: Described as one piece of a very big puzzle, the Caterpillar Tucson Proving Ground (TPG) is now enjoying the benefits of solar energy.
The new commercial offering pairs solar energy, with the reliability of Cat gensets and industry-leading energy storage technology.
Now, just a few steps from the roaring generator room that powers TPG, five acres of rolling desert hills are covered in solar panels.
The solar panels, manufactured by First Solar and installed by Empire Cat, are perfectly positioned to capture the abundant Arizona sun, reducing the genset requirements.
To account for these frequent, sudden and unpredictable drops in energy, the microgrid solution uses bidirectional inverters and energy storage in order to balance out the load.
This hybrid solution will reduce the facility’s diesel fuel use and emissions by 33 percent each year.

technicalDetails.detail: Gas cogeneration plant also provides heating and cooling for roughly 180 buildings.
The heart of the Princeton microgrid is the cogeneration plant (center), which was constructed in 1996.
Operators import or export power based on real-time economics and can disconnect the campus to run as an electrical island during utility outages.
After a roughly 20-minute interruption and turbine restart, the campus islanded at about 13 MW until reconnection shortly before midnight on October 31, 2012.
The gas turbine produces electricity while exhaust heat supplies steam; the district-energy plant also dispatches steam- and electric-driven chillers.
The upgraded microgrid is adding solar, geo-exchange, heat pumps, natural-gas backup generation, and paired hot/cold thermal-storage tanks at TIGER and CUB.

Advanced meters, the power-plant supervisory controller, and thermal-stove controllers exchange surplus-wind availability and customer-use data.
The smart-meter architecture enabled surplus wind sold as residential heat at approximately half the cost of diesel heating in the DOE project design.
DOE reported about 30% power-plant fuel reduction and at least 30% heating-fuel reduction for participating ETS homes across the operating systems; these are program-level figures.
